candidates.direction_name: Reopen The PTX One-K 128x128 Hot-Band Control Branch
ranking_notes: The immediately preceding copy-cadence retune already regressed, so another in-family retune should not stay ranked first.
This branch is orthogonal while still preserving the current 128x128 geometry and split.
The prior PTX measurement was already near the current control run, which makes a quick A/B round plausible.
candidates.hypothesis: Round 1/20 already spent the first budget unit on a bounded retune of the active one-K 128x128 copy cadence, and node_a measured that branch slightly slower at 25.381776 ms versus the 25.325055 ms accepted base. The dominant hot-band kernel still owns about 32.918752 ms of device time with 196 registers per thread, only 16.65% active warps, 47.93% tensor-pipe activity, 7.60% long-scoreboard stall, and 5.75% barrier stall. The PTX one-K control branch keeps the same grouped-row 128x128 grid and hot-band split, but swaps the inner accumulate/export control path. That makes it the cleanest orthogonal next test because a prior PTX measurement already landed at 25.379312 ms, close enough that a small control-path win could be real on this surface.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Hot-band inner-loop control and export overhead in the standard one-K 128x128 path, rather than the peeled 64x384 residual rows or the 64x96 tail.
candidates.risk: Moderate to high. The PTX WMMA path is correctness-sensitive, but it is already present in-tree and the implementation surface should stay small because node_c only needs to route the default hot-band launch onto the existing branch.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the current 25.381776 ms run and the prior PTX 25.379312 ms run
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if the PTX one-K branch does not beat the current 25.381776 ms run cleanly or if it regresses correctness/build despite staying on the same hot-band geometry.

candidates.direction_name: Promote The Existing 256x128 Pivot Hot-Band Kernel
ranking_notes: This is the clearest geometry pivot still sitting in-tree and ready to route.
It is ranked below the PTX branch because the implementation surface and behavior change are both larger.
If the PTX path stays flat, this becomes the next serious family to try.
candidates.hypothesis: If the current 4-warp 128x128 branch has saturated its control path, the next structurally different branch should test geometry, not another wait-order micro-tweak. The dominant kernel is still register-limited to two resident CTAs with only 16.65% active warps, and it launches a 60x50 grid over the first 6400 x 7680 hot-band rows. The in-tree 256x128 pivot family restores an 8-warp CTA while halving the pivot-band CTA count, so it is the cleanest way to test whether CTA geometry and block-count overhead are now the limiting factor on the hot band.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: CTA geometry and block-count overhead on the hot-band region, rather than a pure one-K copy-cadence problem.
candidates.risk: Moderate. The kernel already exists, but routing the default path onto it changes the hot-band family more materially than the PTX branch and could trade lower CTA count for a worse live set.
metrics_to_recheck: hot-band kernel name and grid size in ncu_details.csv
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if the 256x128 pivot branch cannot beat the current one-K 128x128 control run or if its larger CTA shape lowers active warps without improving hot-band time.

candidates.direction_name: Retune Hot-Band CTA Traversal On The 128x128 Grid
ranking_notes: This branch is intentionally cheap and reversible.
It stays below the PTX and 256x128 candidates because prior CTA-order ideas have had mixed evidence.
Keep it alive as the small-surface fallback if both larger hot-band branches stall.
candidates.hypothesis: The current default hot-band branch already remaps physical CTAs into grouped logical tiles, but it still launches a large 60x50 grid while only two 4-warp CTAs can reside at once. In that low-occupancy regime, a lighter grouped-row or serpentine traversal on the 128x128 grid may recover a small amount of inter-CTA B-tile locality without changing the math kernel, registers, or shared-memory footprint. This is a cheap locality probe that stays orthogonal to both the PTX control branch and the 256x128 geometry branch.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Inter-CTA locality and block traversal efficiency on the hot-band grid under register-limited low occupancy.
candidates.risk: Low to moderate. The change surface is small, but previous heavier CTA-order experiments on other hot-band families were not strong wins, so this stays as a lower-priority branch.
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if CTA traversal changes leave hot-band time and L2-facing counters flat after one round; do not spend repeated rounds on launch-order tuning.

candidates.direction_name: Promote The Existing 256x128 Pivot Hot-Band Kernel
ranking_notes: This is now the highest-upside untouched family on top of the new PTX base.
It is intentionally ranked ahead of more PTX-local tuning because round 1/20 already validated the current family.
If this loses cleanly, the search can fall back to tighter exploit passes on the PTX base.
candidates.hypothesis: Round 1/20 established the PTX one-k 128x128 branch as the new accepted base at 24.419329 ms, which already beats CUTLASS. That removes the pressure to keep shaving only the same control path and makes round 2/20 a better place to test the strongest orthogonal family still sitting in-tree. The hot-band PTX kernel is still the wall at 32.687232 ms with 200 registers per thread and only 16.62% active warps while launching a 60x50 grid over the first 6400 x 7680 rows. The existing 256x128 pivot family halves the pivot-band CTA count and restores an 8-warp CTA, so it is the cleanest next test of whether hot-band geometry and block-count overhead are now the limiting factor.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Hot-band CTA geometry and block-count overhead on the current 128x128 PTX base, rather than a remaining local control-path bubble inside the winning branch.
candidates.risk: Moderate. The kernel already exists in-tree, but routing the default path onto it changes the hot-band family more materially than another PTX micro-retune and could lose if the larger CTA worsens the live set.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the new 24.419329 ms base
hot-band kernel name and grid size in ncu_details.csv
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if the 256x128 pivot branch cannot beat the 24.419329 ms PTX base or if it lowers active warps / raises registers without improving hot-band time.

candidates.direction_name: Retune The Active PTX One-K 128x128 Hot-Band Control Path
ranking_notes: Keep one bounded exploit branch alive because the PTX family is now the accepted winner.
This is ranked below 256x128 because the geometric branch is the stronger orthogonal test at the current state.
If the pivot family loses, this becomes the next likely best move.
candidates.hypothesis: The PTX one-k branch just delivered a large win, which is evidence that this family is the right base even if it may still carry some local waste. The hot-band kernel still runs at 32.687232 ms with 200 registers per thread, 7.20% long-scoreboard stall, and 5.41% barrier stall. A bounded exploit pass inside the PTX family should focus only on the PTX accumulate/export and one-k handoff path, not on changing geometry again. That makes it the best fallback if the 256x128 pivot family fails to capitalize on its theoretical block-count advantage.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Residual control-path overhead and live-range pressure inside the winning PTX one-k 128x128 hot-band branch.
candidates.risk: Moderate. This stays on the best-known family, but PTX microkernel changes are correctness-sensitive and the remaining upside is probably narrower than the geometry branch.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the 24.419329 ms base
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if a first PTX-local retune does not improve hot-band time or if it raises registers further without a runtime gain.

candidates.direction_name: Retune Hot-Band CTA Traversal On The 128x128 PTX Grid
ranking_notes: This stays alive because it is the cheapest reversible probe on the current best family.
It remains below the other two because the likely upside is smaller and the evidence is weaker.
Use it only if the larger geometry and PTX-local branches both flatten.
candidates.hypothesis: The winning PTX base still launches a large 60x50 hot-band grid while only two 4-warp CTAs can stay resident per SM. In that low-occupancy regime, a small grouped-row or serpentine traversal remap may recover some inter-CTA locality without changing the mathematical kernel, shared-memory footprint, or CTA size. This remains the cheap, low-surface fallback branch if both the 256x128 pivot and a bounded PTX exploit pass fail to move the frontier.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Inter-CTA locality and traversal efficiency on the current 128x128 PTX hot-band grid.
candidates.risk: Low to moderate. The surface is small, but previous CTA-order experiments on other hot-band families were mixed, so the upside is bounded.
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if CTA traversal changes leave hot-band time and cache-facing counters flat after one round.

candidates.direction_name: Retune The Active PTX One-K 128x128 Hot-Band Control Path
ranking_notes: This matches the current highest-scoring open family representative in the frontier after the 256x128 loss.
It restores focus to the last accepted base instead of spending another round on a broad geometry hypothesis.
Keep the scope bounded to one PTX control-path improvement, not a fresh rewrite.
candidates.hypothesis: Round 2/20 falsified the 256x128 geometry pivot hard: end-to-end runtime regressed from the accepted 24.419329 ms PTX base to 30.286848 ms, and the hot-band kernel alone grew to 43.291456 ms even though registers fell to 168/thread. That means the best next move is not another wide family jump but a bounded exploit pass on top of the accepted PTX one-k 128x128 branch. The winning PTX base still had 200 registers per thread, only 16.62% active warps, 7.20% long-scoreboard stall, and 5.41% barrier stall, so there is still room to trim local control/export overhead without repeating the failed geometry experiment.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Residual control-path overhead and live-range pressure inside the accepted PTX one-k 128x128 hot-band branch.
candidates.risk: Moderate. PTX microkernel changes are correctness-sensitive, but this stays on the last measured winner instead of introducing another new geometry family.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the accepted 24.419329 ms PTX base
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if one PTX-local retune cannot improve hot-band time or if it raises register pressure without recovering runtime.

candidates.direction_name: Retune Hot-Band CTA Traversal On The 128x128 PTX Grid
ranking_notes: This is the best low-surface explore family still open after the geometry loss.
It remains below the PTX exploit path because the likely upside is smaller.
Use it only if the exploit pass does not recover anything.
candidates.hypothesis: After the failed 256x128 pivot, the safest remaining orthogonal test is a small traversal/locality probe on the proven PTX base. The accepted PTX branch still launches a 60x50 hot-band grid while only two 4-warp CTAs can stay resident per SM. A grouped-row or serpentine traversal tweak is cheap, reversible, and orthogonal to PTX inner-loop control retuning, so it stays alive as the next best explore family if the bounded PTX exploit path stalls.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Inter-CTA locality and traversal efficiency on the current 128x128 PTX hot-band grid.
candidates.risk: Low to moderate. The surface is small, but prior CTA-order experiments have shown mixed evidence, so the upside is limited.
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if traversal changes leave hot-band time and cache-facing counters flat after one round.

candidates.direction_name: Restore The Accepted PTX One-K 128x128 Base
ranking_notes: Keep one explicit recovery candidate in the set because the current source is far off the accepted base.
This is intentionally ranked below forward-looking directions because it mostly preserves state instead of exploring new upside.
If round-to-round noise or build risk rises, this becomes the safety valve.
candidates.hypothesis: The current source surface is the losing 256x128 branch, while the accepted PTX base at 24.419329 ms is already known-good and already beats CUTLASS. If the loop needs to recover a safe surface before any further exploration, routing the default hot-band launch straight back to the accepted PTX one-k branch is the lowest-risk move. This stays as the third direction because it does not push the frontier forward by itself, but it is still a valid option if the loop starts accumulating noisy losses.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Not a new bottleneck attack; this is the recovery path back to the accepted PTX base after the failed 256x128 excursion.
candidates.risk: Low. This is a simple return to the last measured winner.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the accepted 24.419329 ms run
candidates.stop_condition: Use this if the higher-ranked directions look too risky or if the loop needs to get back onto the accepted PTX base immediately.

candidates.direction_name: Retune Hot-Band CTA Traversal On The 128x128 PTX Grid
ranking_notes: This is the cheapest orthogonal family still open on top of the current best PTX base.
It is ranked ahead of another PTX control tweak because the latest exploit win did not materially change hot-band counters.
If this stays flat, the search can return to PTX-local tuning with little regret.
candidates.hypothesis: Round 3/20 recovered from the 256x128 loss and set a new best at 24.177664 ms, but the hot-band PTX kernel's NCU signature barely moved relative to the previous PTX base: kernel time remained about 32.687 ms and register count stayed at 200/thread. That suggests the unroll tweak was only a small perturbation rather than a structural feed breakthrough. The best next move is therefore a cheap orthogonal traversal/locality test on the proven PTX base. The current branch still launches a 60x50 hot-band grid while only two 4-warp CTAs can stay resident per SM, so a grouped-row or serpentine traversal remap is the best low-risk probe of inter-CTA locality.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Inter-CTA locality and traversal efficiency on the current 128x128 PTX hot-band grid under low occupancy.
candidates.risk: Low to moderate. The surface is small and reversible, but previous CTA-order experiments have had mixed evidence, so the upside is bounded.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the 24.177664 ms base
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if one traversal tweak leaves hot-band time and cache-facing counters flat after a single measured round.

candidates.direction_name: Continue The Active PTX One-K 128x128 Control-Path Exploit
ranking_notes: Keep one live exploit family because it is still the current best performer.
This drops below traversal because the last measured win barely changed the hot-band signature.
If traversal is flat, this is still the next most coherent family.
candidates.hypothesis: The PTX exploit family is still healthy because it just set the best run, even if the measured counters barely moved. A second bounded exploit pass remains worth keeping in the set in case traversal changes also land flat. The next PTX-local tweak should stay tightly scoped to copy/order or export control in the PTX one-k hot-band kernel, not reopen geometry or shared-footprint changes.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Residual PTX control-path overhead in the current one-k 128x128 hot-band branch.
candidates.risk: Moderate. This stays on the best-known family, but repeated PTX micro-tweaks can easily devolve into noise if the counters stop moving.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the 24.177664 ms base
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if another PTX-local tweak also leaves the hot-band counters effectively unchanged.

candidates.direction_name: Reopen The Prior PTX One-K 128x128 Control Branch For A/B Guardrail
ranking_notes: Keep one explicit PTX-family guardrail because the current best and the prior PTX baseline are close enough that future noise matters.
This is below the forward-looking directions because it mainly preserves comparison quality.
Use it if the loop needs a clean PTX A/B reset.
candidates.hypothesis: The current best source is a PTX branch with the unroll-1 tweak layered on top. If the next rounds become noisy, the cleanest A/B control is to reopen the prior PTX one-k baseline that already measured 24.419329 ms. That keeps a known-good comparison point in the candidate set without reintroducing the failed 256x128 geometry family.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Not a new bottleneck attack; this is the PTX-family A/B restore guardrail.
candidates.risk: Low. This is a return to a previously measured PTX baseline variant.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the current 24.177664 ms base and the prior 24.419329 ms PTX run
candidates.stop_condition: Use this only if the higher-ranked directions become noisy or need a clean PTX-family control run.
